Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it never really worked out that way. I'd log into the LMS, watch some videos, maybe start reading something, but then I'd get distracted or overwhelmed and just... drift. I spent a lot of time on the platform without actually accomplishing much structured studying. It's frustrating because I wanted to be more organized, but I just couldn't seem to make it stick.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd be really active, especially when something caught my attention or when I felt that spark of motivation. But then other weeks, especially when I had work stuff going on or was just feeling anxious about everything, I'd barely touch anything until a deadline forced me to. I never really established a consistent schedule even though I knew I should have.
Q3: Usually it was either boredom or panic that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd just be scrolling on my phone and think, 'I should probably check the LMS,' and then I'd end up watching videos for a while. Other times it was pure deadline anxiety - like realizing an assignment was due and frantically logging in to figure out what I needed to do. The gamified tasks actually drew me in sometimes too; those little challenges felt less intimidating than regular work.
Q4: For a while, logging in became almost like a nervous habit for me. I'd check it multiple times a day, not really doing anything productive, just... looking around. Watching the same videos again, checking discussion posts. It became routine in the sense that I was always there, but not routine in a productive way. The gamified elements helped make it feel less like a chore, so I kept coming back even when I wasn't completing actual assignments.
Q5: The videos were my lifeline, honestly. I'm a visual learner, so being able to watch explanations over and over really helped concepts click for me. I spent over four hours just on videos because I could pause, rewind, and really try to understand things at my own pace. The gamified tasks were also helpful because they made learning feel less overwhelming and more like something I could actually accomplish in small chunks.
Q6: I basically ignored the peer interaction stuff completely. Zero peer interactions - I know that looks bad. I just felt too anxious to put myself out there and potentially say something stupid. The discussion posts I made were minimal and I always stressed over them. I also delayed a lot of the reading materials because walls of text just make my brain shut down. I'd open them, feel overwhelmed, and close them to 'come back later.'
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate progress or when it felt manageable. The gamified tasks were great for this - I could see my progress, earn something, feel like I accomplished something small. Videos felt worth it because I was actively learning something visual. But long reading assignments or complex problems? Those felt like black holes where my time would disappear with nothing to show for it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a relief, honestly. There was no pressure, so I could explore them without the anxiety of 'what if I mess this up.' Required ones made my chest tight just thinking about them. I'd often do optional things to avoid the required ones, which I know is backwards. It's like the stakes being lower made me more willing to actually engage rather than freeze up.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and PDFs to my tablet so I could look at them without being online. Sometimes I'd screenshot diagrams from videos and study those separately. I also used YouTube to find other explanations of concepts when the course materials weren't clicking. My notes app is full of stuff I typed out while watching videos, but none of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were so many times I'd be lying in bed going over concepts in my head, or looking at my downloaded notes on the bus. I'd rewatch parts of videos I'd already marked as 'completed' because I needed to see them again. Sometimes I'd spend an hour trying to understand one diagram I'd saved to my phone. The LMS only knows I watched the video once, but I probably reviewed certain parts five or six times offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd download the week's materials in one session, and then study from those files for days without logging back in. It was easier to have everything on my tablet where I could annotate and highlight without needing internet. So the LMS would show one access, but I was actually using those materials repeatedly throughout the week. It just wasn't tracked.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart for me. My anxiety spiked really badly, and I started having trouble sleeping. The workload from all seven of my online courses felt crushing, and I just... shut down. I'd log in, stare at the screen, watch a video without really absorbing anything, and then log out feeling worse than before. It was like being paralyzed - I could see what I needed to do but couldn't make myself do it.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd still log into the LMS because I felt guilty not doing anything, but I wasn't really there mentally. I'd let videos play while my mind wandered. Having seven online courses was way too much - I was constantly juggling and dropping balls. The confusion from falling behind made the anxiety worse, which made it harder to engage, which made me fall further behind. It was a vicious cycle.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the LMS open for hours, technically 'studying,' but really just going through the motions. Clicking through slides without reading them, letting videos play while I checked my phone, opening discussion threads and then immediately closing them. My engagement level might look high from the outside, but a lot of that time was me being present without being mentally there at all.
Q15: I avoided assignments constantly, even though I knew they were destroying my grade. The anxiety around them was so intense that I'd do literally anything else - watch more videos, do optional quizzes, organize my notes - just to avoid starting the actual work. It's not that I didn't care; I cared so much that the fear of failing made it impossible to even begin. My assignment completion rate is terrible and I hate that about myself.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and the only thing that got me moving. Without a deadline, I'd drift endlessly. With a deadline, I'd panic-work at the last minute, usually producing something rushed and incomplete. I'd log in way more frequently as deadlines approached, but by then it was often too late to do quality work. The 18-hour average response time probably reflects all those times I saw something was due and scrambled to respond.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but not always positively. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback sent my anxiety through the roof. Sometimes I'd avoid checking my grades for days because I couldn't handle the potential disappointment. The gamified feedback was easier to take - it felt less personal somehow. Regular assignment feedback felt like a judgment on me as a person.
Q18: The course structure was overwhelming with how much content there was. Having seven courses meant I could never give this one the attention it probably needed. The weekly modules were clear enough, but I'd fall behind and then the backlog would feel insurmountable. The gamified elements helped break things into smaller pieces, which I appreciated, but the overall structure still felt like too much for someone already struggling with anxiety.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier - they gave me small wins when everything else felt impossible. The video content being available to rewatch was crucial for my learning style. What made it harder was the assignment structure - they felt too big and intimidating. Also, the lack of flexibility when I was having bad mental health days meant I'd fall behind with no way to catch up. More scaffolding on the big assignments would have helped me actually complete them.
Q20: That's literally me, isn't it? I think it happens when you're trying to engage but can't convert that effort into finished work. For me, anxiety made starting assignments feel impossible, so I'd do everything else - watch videos, check discussions, do practice quizzes - as a way of feeling productive without facing the scary stuff. High activity can mask avoidance behaviors. You're present but not producing because something is blocking you.
Q21: I can see how that would work for some people. If you're efficient, focused, and know what you need, you could probably learn everything from a few targeted sessions and then study offline. I wish I could be like that. For me, my high activity didn't translate to performance because I wasn't being strategic - I was being anxious and scattered. But someone with better focus could definitely do more with less visible activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you're clicking on, how long you spend on things. That's useful for identifying who's completely absent. But they miss everything about quality of engagement. They can't tell if you're actually understanding the video you're watching or just letting it play. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the anxiety that's preventing someone from submitting work they've actually completed.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, when you're thinking about the content even outside of study time, when you want to understand rather than just pass. I was engaged in that sense - I genuinely wanted to learn and spent so much time trying. But my engagement didn't translate to success because anxiety kept sabotaging my ability to complete and submit work.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I was struggling internally while appearing active externally. It can't capture the hours I spent paralyzed by anxiety, unable to start assignments I actually understood. It doesn't know about my panic attacks before deadlines or how the gamified tasks were sometimes the only thing keeping me connected to the course at all. It sees my failure but not the fight I was putting up just to stay partially afloat.
